title: Moiré enabled spin pumping and preservation in MoSe2/WS2 heterobilayers
authors: Leo Yu, Kateryna Pistunova, Sudipta Kundu, Jenny Hu, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Felipe H. da Jornada, Tony F. Heinz
abstract: The spin degree of freedom is a fundamental quantum mechanical attribute with implications spanning from magnetism to quantum computing. Consequently, the relaxation of spin states for extended, Bloch electrons in solids has been studied for decades as it defines many of their properties and applications. We show that moiré patterns in layered materials can extend spin relaxation times by two orders of magnitude to 1 millisecond and beyond. This is achieved by suppressing spin mixing for electrons in 2D semiconductor heterostructures, particularly in the MoSe2/WS2 system, as we elucidate both experimentally and theoretically. The extended longitudinal lifetime facilitates spin alignment over 50% using only nanowatt levels of optical power. Our findings highlight the potential of moiré engineering for future quantum sensing and information processing.
summary: This work demonstrates that creating moiré patterns in $	ext{MoSe}_2/	ext{WS}_2$ heterobilayers dramatically extends the spin relaxation time of electrons, a crucial property for quantum technologies. By combining optical measurements with detailed theoretical modeling, the authors show that moiré confinement suppresses spin mixing, enabling long-lived spin coherence at low power levels.
main_problem: The research aims to understand and significantly extend the spin relaxation times (SRTs) of Bloch electrons in solid-state systems by engineering moiré patterns in van der Waals heterobilayers.
main_result: Moiré engineering in $	ext{MoSe}_2/	ext{WS}_2$ heterobilayers suppresses spin relaxation by orders of magnitude compared to monolayers, enabling long spin coherence times necessary for quantum applications.
method: The study combines advanced optical spectroscopy (TRLE, RHC) to measure spin dynamics with theoretical modeling ($\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{p}$ and DFT) to explain the underlying physics.
model_or_system: The physical system is the $	ext{MoSe}_2/	ext{WS}_2$ heterobilayer, which forms moiré supercells. The electronic structure is analyzed using $\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{p}$ theory, incorporating moiré potentials derived from DFT calculations.
key_observables: Spin relaxation time ($	au$), valley polarization, reflection helicity contrast (RHC) spectra, and time-resolved light ellipticity modulation.
important_parameters: Moiré periodicity, carrier density ($n$), temperature, and optical power levels.
main_assumptions: The long-lived TRLE signal is attributed to valley polarization/spin pumping, and the spin relaxation mechanism in the HBL is linked to the Elliott-Yafet mechanism mediated by impurity scattering.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the moiré lattice structure, doping-dependent optical spectra showing valley polarization, and time-resolved measurements demonstrating the vastly extended spin coherence time in the heterobilayer compared to the monolayer.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first characterizing the electronic structure and valley polarization in the HBL vs. ML using spectroscopy, then quantifying the spin relaxation time using TRLE, and finally providing theoretical explanations based on moiré band structure calculations.
why_interesting: The ability to engineer spin coherence times in solid-state systems using moiré potentials is highly relevant to quantum information, as it offers a platform for realizing long-lived spin qubits or quantum memories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. a, Moiré superlattice formed in angle-aligned MoSe2/WS2 heterobilayers with the superlattice cell outlined in black. The high-symmetry stackings are identified as 2H, MoW, and SeS, also referred to as AA’, AB’, and A’B, respectively. b, The dual-gated device structure of the HBL. c, Doping-dependent reflection contrast spectra of a MoSe2 ML feature the exciton and the trion peak. The dashed line marks the doping level at which subsequent helicity-contrasting spectra were recorded. d, Doping-dependent reflection contrast spectra of the HBL, showing distinct spectral feature changes at integer fillings of moiré sites (dashed lines). e, f, Reflective helicity contrast (RHC) spectra...

Figure 2. Time-resolved light ellipticity in heterobilayer and monolayer MoSe2. a, Temporal profiles of probe-light counts from the heterobilayer (HBL) under various configurations of pump and probe light. In the pump-only trace, the peak near time zero is attributed to the PL from the trion resonance, lasting for the duration of the pump light (i.e., 1 μs). The photon energy of the pump light was 1.64 eV. The filling factor of the HBL was set at 0.8. Bottom panel: Fractional change in the probe light intensity, normalized to the level when the pump light is off. b, Fractional change in the probe light intensity following rapid spin relaxation in the ML, measured in nanoseconds. Figure 3. a, The fractional change in the probe light is shown as a function of the time delay after the pump pulse at five representative temperatures, with a representative filling factor of 0.2 in the low doping regime and with a probe power of 5 nW. b, Temperature dependence of the spin relaxation rate. The Arrhenius fit yields an activation energy of 17 meV. c, The same quantity as in a is shown for three representative filling factors, measured at a probe power of 5 nW. d, Doping dependence of the spin relaxation rate. The CBM is at 𝐊୑, where the effective mass roughly doubles. b, c, Projection amplitudes onto the 𝑣↓ (b) and 𝑣−1 ↑ (c) band for a wavefunction near CBM, as a function of carrier momentum along 𝐊୑−𝚪୑ (bottom axis) or moiré filling factor (top axis). Compared to the ML result (gray line), the HBL results are labeled with the mini-band index (1, 4, 7) and lower in magnitude (see text). d, Biaxial strain distribution in the MoSe2 part of the HBL over a moiré supercell from lattice reconstruction. Spintronics.note: The paper directly addresses spin dynamics (spin pumping, spin relaxation time) in a solid-state heterostructure ($\text{MoSe}_2/\text{WS}_2$), which falls squarely within the scope of spintronics, particularly concerning spin transport in engineered materials.
